title: Divide multi-digit numbers by 6, 7, 8, and 9 (remainders)
description: Practice dividing 2-, 3-, and 4-digit numbers by a 1-digit number.  Divisors are 6, 7, 8, and 9.  Quotients include remainders.
topic_path: Topic G: Division of thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones

hints:
To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $6$ that may be useful:

- $6 \times 3 = 18$
- $6 \times 5 = 30$
- $6 \times 10 = 60$

Let's start by dividing $2{,}167$ into $\purpleD{300}$ sixes.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{6}&\overline{\Big)2{,}167}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{1{,}800}}&\purpleD{300}&\maroonD{6} \times \purpleD{300} = \blueE{1{,}800}\\
&367
\end{array}$

We have $367$ left.

Let's divide $367$ into $\purpleD{50}$ more sixes.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{6}&\overline{\Big)2{,}167}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}800}&300\\
&367\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{300}}&\purpleD{50}&\maroonD{6} \times \purpleD{50} = \blueE{300}\\
&67
\end{array}$

We have $67$ left.

Let's divide $67$ into $\purpleD{10}$ more sixes.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{6}&\overline{\Big)2{,}167}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}800}&300\\
&367\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{300}&50\\
&67\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{60}}&\purpleD{10}&\maroonD{6} \times \purpleD{10} = \blueE{60}\\
&7
\end{array}$

We have $7$ left.

Let's divide $7$ into $\purpleD{1}$ more six.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{6}&\overline{\Big)2{,}167}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}800}&300\\
&367\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{300}&50\\
&67\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{60}&10\\
&7\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{6}}&\purpleD{1}&\maroonD{6} \times \purpleD{1} = \blueE{6}\\
&1
\end{array}$

We have $1$ left.  That is less than $6$, so we'll stop dividing.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{6}&\overline{\Big)2{,}167}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}800}&\purpleD{300}\\
&367\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{300}&\purpleD{50}\\
&67\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{60}&\purpleD{10}\\
&7\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{1}}\\
&\goldE{1}&\greenE{361}
\end{array}$

$\purpleE{300} + \purpleE{50} + \purpleE{10} + \purpleE{1}= \greenE{361}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{1}$.

$\begin{align}
\greenE{361}&\text{ remainder }\goldE{1}\\
6\overline{\Big)2{,}167}&
\end{align}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $7$ that may be useful:

- $7 \times 3 = 21$
- $7 \times 4 = 28$
- $7 \times 5 = 35$

Let's start by dividing $276$ into $\purpleD{30}$ sevens.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{7}&\overline{\Big)276}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{210}}&\purpleD{30}&\maroonD{7} \times \purpleD{30} = \blueE{210}\\
&66
\end{array}$

We have $66$ left.

Let's divide $66$ into $\purpleD{9}$ more sevens because $9\times 7=63$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{7}&\overline{\Big)276}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{210}&30\\
&66\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{63}}&\purpleD{9}&\maroonD{7} \times \purpleD{9} = \blueE{63}\\
&3
\end{array}$

We have $3$ left. That is less than $7$, so we'll stop dividing.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{7}&\overline{\Big)276}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{210}&\purpleD{30}\\
&66\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{63}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{9}}\\
&\goldE{3}&\greenE{39}
\end{array}$

$\purpleE{30} + \purpleE{9}= \greenE{39}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{3}$.
$276 \div 7 =\greenE{39}$ remainder $\goldE{3}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $9$ that may be useful:

- $9 \times 3 = 27$
- $9 \times 4 = 36$
- $9 \times 5 = 45$

Let's start by dividing $393$ into $\purpleD{40}$ nines.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{9}&\overline{\Big)393}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{360}}&\purpleD{40}&\maroonD{9} \times \purpleD{40} = \blueE{360}\\
&33
\end{array}$

We have $33$ left.

Let's divide $33$ into $\purpleD{3}$ more nines.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{9}&\overline{\Big)393}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{360}&40\\
&33\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{27}}&\purpleD{3}&\maroonD{9} \times \purpleD{3} = \blueE{27}\\
&6
\end{array}$

We have $6$ left.  That is less than $9$, so we'll stop dividing.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{9}&\overline{\Big)393}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{360}&\purpleD{40}\\
&33\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{27}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{3}}\\
&\goldE{6}&\greenE{43}
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{40} + \purpleD{3}= \greenE{43}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{6}$.

$\begin{align}
\greenE{43}&\text{ remainder }\goldE{6}\\
9\overline{\Big)393}&
\end{align}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $8$ that may be useful:

- $8 \times 2 = 16$
- $8 \times 7 = 56$
- $8 \times 10 = 80$

Let's start by dividing $5{,}698$ into $\purpleD{700}$ eights.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{8}&\overline{\Big)5{,}698}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{5{,}600}}&\purpleD{700}&\maroonD{8} \times \purpleD{700} = \blueE{5{,}600}\\
&98
\end{array}$

We have $98$ left.

Let's divide $98$ into $\purpleD{10}$ more eights.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{8}&\overline{\Big)5{,}698}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{5{,}600}&700\\
&98\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{80}}&\purpleD{10}&\maroonD{8} \times \purpleD{10} = \blueE{80}\\
&18
\end{array}$

We have $18$ left.

Let's divide $18$ into $\purpleD{2}$ more eights.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{8}&\overline{\Big)5{,}698}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{5{,}600}&700\\
&98\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{80}&10\\
&18\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{16}}&\purpleD{2}&\maroonD{8} \times \purpleD{2} = \blueE{16}\\
&2
\end{array}$

We have $2$ left.  That is less than $8$, so we'll stop dividing.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{8}&\overline{\Big)5{,}698}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{5{,}600}&\purpleD{700}\\
&98\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{80}&\purpleD{10}\\
&18\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{16}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{2}}\\
&\goldE{2}&\greenE{712}
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{700} + \purpleD{10} + \purpleD{2}= \greenE{712}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{2}$.
$5{,}698 \div 8 = \greenE{712}$ remainder $\goldE{2}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $8$ that may be useful:

- $8 \times 2 = 16$
- $8 \times 5 = 40$
- $8 \times 10 = 80$

Let's start by dividing $200$ into $\purpleD{20}$ eights.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{8}&\overline{\Big)200}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{160}}&\purpleD{20}&\maroonD{8} \times \purpleD{20} = \blueE{160}\\
&40\\
\end{array}$

We have $40$ left.

Let's divide $40$ into $\purpleD{5}$ more eights since  $5\times8=40$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{8}&\overline{\Big)200}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{160}&20\\
&40\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{40}}&\purpleD{5}&\maroonD{8} \times \purpleD{5} = \blueE{40}\\
&0\\
\end{array}$

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{8}&\overline{\Big)200}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{160}&\purpleD{20}\\
&40\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{40}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{5}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{25}
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{20} + \purpleD{5}= \greenE{25}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{0}$.
$200 \div 8 =\greenE{25}$ remainder $\goldE{0}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $7$ that may be useful:

- $7 \times 7 = 49$
- $7 \times 8 = 56$
- $7 \times 10 = 70$

Let's start by dividing $7{,}055$ into $\purpleD{1{,}000}$ sevens.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{7}&\overline{\Big)7{,}055}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{7{,}000}}&\purpleD{1{,}000}&\maroonD{7} \times \purpleD{1000} = \blueE{7{,}000}\\
&55
\end{array}$

We have $55$ left.

Let's divide $55$ into $\purpleD{7}$ more sevens.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{7}&\overline{\Big)7{,}055}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{7{,}000}&1{,}000\\
&55\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{49}}&\purpleD{7}&\maroonD{7} \times \purpleD{7} = \blueE{49}\\
&6
\end{array}$

We have $6$ left. That is less than $7$, so we'll stop dividing.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{7}&\overline{\Big)7{,}055}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{7{,}000}&\purpleD{1{,}000}\\
&55\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{49}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{7}}\\
&\goldE{6}&\greenE{1{,}007}
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{1{,}000} + \purpleD{7}= \greenE{1{,}007}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{6}$.
$7{,}055 \div 7 =\greenE{1{,}007}$ remainder $\goldE{6}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $6$ that may be useful:

- $6 \times 2 = 12$
- $6 \times 5 = 30$
- $6 \times 10 = 60$

Let's start by dividing $906$ into $\purpleD{100}$ sixes.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{6}&\overline{\Big)906}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{600}}&\purpleD{100}&\maroonD{6} \times \purpleD{100} = \blueE{600}\\
&306
\end{array}$

We have $306$ left.

Let's divide $306$ into $\purpleD{50}$ more sixes.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{6}&\overline{\Big)906}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{600}&100\\
&306\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{300}}&\purpleD{50}&\maroonD{6} \times \purpleD{50} = \blueE{300}\\
&6\\
\end{array}$

We have $6$ left.

Let's divide $6$ into $\purpleD{1}$ more six.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{6}&\overline{\Big)906}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{600}&100\\
&306\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{300}&50\\
&6\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{6}}&\purpleD{1}&\maroonD{6} \times \purpleD{1} = \blueE{6}\\
&0
\end{array}$

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{6}&\overline{\Big)906}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{600}&\purpleD{100}\\
&306\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{300}&\purpleD{50}\\
&6\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{1}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{151}
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{100} + \purpleD{50} + \purpleD{1}= \greenE{151}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{0}$.

$\begin{align}
\greenE{151}&\text{ remainder }\goldE{0}\\
6\overline{\Big)906}&
\end{align}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $9$ that may be useful:

- $9 \times 2 = 18$
- $9 \times 4 = 36$
- $9 \times 5 = 45$

Let's start by dividing $4{,}008$ into $\purpleD{400}$ nines.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{9}&\overline{\Big)4{,}008}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{3{,}600}}&\purpleD{400}&\maroonD{9} \times \purpleD{400} = \blueE{3{,}600}\\
&408
\end{array}$

We have $408$ left.

Let's divide $408$ into $\purpleD{40}$ more nines.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{9}&\overline{\Big)4{,}008}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{3{,}600}&400\\
&408\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{360}}&\purpleD{40}&\maroonD{9} \times \purpleD{40} = \blueE{360}\\
&48
\end{array}$

We have $48$ left.

Let's divide $48$ into $\purpleD{5}$ more nines.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{9}&\overline{\Big)4{,}008}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{3{,}600}&400\\
&408\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{360}&40\\
&48\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{45}}&\purpleD{5}&\maroonD{9} \times \purpleD{5} = \blueE{45}\\
&3
\end{array}$

We have $3$ left.  That is less than $9$, so we'll stop dividing.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{9}&\overline{\Big)4{,}008}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{3{,}600}&\purpleD{400}\\
&408\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{360}&\purpleD{40}\\
&48\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{45}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{5}}\\
&\goldE{3}&\greenE{445}
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{400} + \purpleD{40} + \purpleD{5}= \greenE{445}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{3}$.

$\begin{align}
\greenE{445}&\text{ remainder }\goldE{3}\\
9\overline{\Big)4{,}008}&
\end{align}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $9$ that may be useful:

- $9 \times 3 = 27$
- $9 \times 4 = 36$
- $9 \times 10 = 90$

Let's start by dividing $135$ into $\purpleD{10}$ nines.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{9}&\overline{\Big)135}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{90}}&\purpleD{10}&\maroonD{9} \times \purpleD{10} = \blueE{90}\\
&45
\end{array}$

We have $45$ left.

Let's divide $45$ into $\purpleD{5}$ more nines since  $5\times9=45$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{9}&\overline{\Big)135}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{90}&10\\
&45\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{45}}&\purpleD{5}&\maroonD{9} \times \purpleD{5} = \blueE{45}\\
&0
\end{array}$

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{9}&\overline{\Big)135}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{90}&\purpleD{10}\\
&45\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{45}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{5}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{15}
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{10} + \purpleD{5}= \greenE{15}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{0}$.
$135 \div 9 =\greenE{15}$ remainder $\goldE{0}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $6$ that may be useful:

- $6 \times 4 = 24$
- $6 \times 5 = 30$
- $6 \times 10 = 60$

Let's start by dividing $8{,}466$ into $\purpleD{1{,}000}$ sixes.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{6}&\overline{\Big)8{,}466}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{6{,}000}}&\purpleD{1{,}000}&\maroonD{6} \times \purpleD{1{,}000} = \blueE{6{,}000}\\
&2{,}466
\end{array}$

We have $2{,}466$ left.

Let's divide $2{,}466$ into $\purpleD{400}$ more sixes.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{6}&\overline{\Big)8{,}466}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6{,}000}&1{,}000\\
&2{,}466\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{2{,}400}}&\purpleD{400}&\maroonD{6} \times \purpleD{400} = \blueE{2{,}400}\\
&66
\end{array}$

We have $66$ left.

Let's divide $66$ into $\purpleD{10}$ more sixes.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{6}&\overline{\Big)8{,}466}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6{,}000}&1{,}000\\
&2{,}466\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{2{,}400}&400\\
&66\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{60}}&\purpleD{10}&\maroonD{6} \times \purpleD{10} = \blueE{60}\\
&6
\end{array}$

We have $6$ left.

Let's divide $6$ into $\purpleD{1}$ more six.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{6}&\overline{\Big)8{,}466}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6{,}000}&1{,}000\\
&2{,}466\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{2{,}400}&400\\
&66\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{60}&10\\
&6\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{6}}&\purpleD{1}&\maroonD{6} \times \purpleD{1} = \blueE{6}\\
&0
\end{array}$

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{6}&\overline{\Big)8{,}466}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6{,}000}&\purpleD{1{,}000}\\
&2{,}466\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{2{,}400}&\purpleD{400}\\
&66\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{60}&\purpleD{10}\\
&6\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{1}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{1{,}411}
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{1000} + \purpleD{400} + \purpleD{10} + \purpleD{1}= \greenE{1{,}411}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{0}$.
$8{,}466 \div 6 =\greenE{1{,}411}$ remainder $\goldE{0}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $8$ that may be useful:

- $8 \times 10 = 80$
- $8 \times 2 = 16$
- $8 \times 5 = 40$

Let's start by dividing $392$ into $\purpleD{20}$ eights.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{8}&\overline{\Big)392}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{160}}&\purpleD{20}&\maroonD{8} \times \purpleD{20} = \blueE{160}\\
&232
\end{array}$

We have $232$ left.

Let's divide $232$ into $\purpleD{20}$ more eights.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{8}&\overline{\Big)392}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{160}&20\\
&232\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{160}}&\purpleD{20}&\maroonD{8} \times \purpleD{20} = \blueE{160}\\
&72
\end{array}$

We have $72$ left.

Let's divide $72$ into $\purpleD{9}$ more eights since  $8\times9=72$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{8}&\overline{\Big)392}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{160}&20\\
&232\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{160}&20\\
&72\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{72}}&\purpleD{9}&\maroonD{8} \times \purpleD{9} = \blueE{72}\\
&0
\end{array}$

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{8}&\overline{\Big)392}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{160}&\purpleD{20}\\
&232\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{160}&\purpleD{20}\\
&72\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{72}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{9}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{49}
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{20}+ \purpleD{20} + \purpleD9 = \greenE{49}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{0}$.
$8\overline{\Big)392} = \greenE{49} $ remainder $\goldE{0}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $7$ that may be useful:

- $7 \times 3 = 21$
- $7 \times 5 = 35$
- $7 \times 9 = 63$

Let's start by dividing $2{,}758$ into $\purpleD{300}$ sevens.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{7}&\overline{\Big)2{,}758}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{2{,}100}}&\purpleD{300}&\maroonD{7} \times \purpleD{300} = \blueE{2{,}100}\\
&658
\end{array}$

We have $658$ left.

Let's divide $658$ into $\purpleD{90}$ more sevens.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{7}&\overline{\Big)2{,}758}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{2{,}100}&300\\
&658\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{630}}&\purpleD{90}&\maroonD{7} \times \purpleD{90} = \blueE{630}\\
&28
\end{array}$

We have $28$ left.

Let's divide $28$ into $\purpleD{4}$ more sevens since  $4\times7=28$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{7}&\overline{\Big)2{,}758}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{2{,}100}&300\\
&658\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{630}&90\\
&28\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{28}}&\purpleD{4}&\maroonD{7} \times \purpleD{4} = \blueE{28}\\
&0
\end{array}$

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{7}&\overline{\Big)2{,}758}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{2{,}100}&\purpleD{300}\\
&658\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{630}&\purpleD{90}\\
&28\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{28}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{4}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{394}\\
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{300} + \purpleD{90} + \purpleD{4}= \greenE{394}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{0}$.
$7\overline{\Big)2{,}758} =  \greenE{394} $ remainder $\goldE{0}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $9$ that may be useful:

- $9 \times 2 = 18$
- $9 \times 4 = 36$
- $9 \times 10 = 90$

Let's start by dividing $3{,}618$ into $\purpleD{400}$ nines.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{9}&\overline{\Big)3{,}618}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{3{,}600}}&\purpleD{400}&\maroonD{9} \times \purpleD{400} = \blueE{3{,}600}\\
&18
\end{array}$

We have $18$ left.

Let's divide $18$ into $\purpleD{2}$ more nines since  $2\times9=18$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{9}&\overline{\Big)3{,}618}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{3{,}600}&400\\
&18\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{18}}&\purpleD{2}&\maroonD{9} \times \purpleD{2} = \blueE{18}\\
&0
\end{array}$

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{9}&\overline{\Big)3{,}618}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{3{,}600}&\purpleD{400}\\
&18\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{18}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{2}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{402}
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{400} + \purpleD{2}= \greenE{402}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{0}$.

$\begin{align}
\greenE{402}&\text{ remainder }\goldE{0}\\
9\overline{\Big)3{,}618}&
\end{align}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $7$ that may be useful:

- $7 \times 5 = 35$
- $7 \times 6 = 42$
- $7 \times 10 = 70$

Let's start by dividing $741$ into $\purpleD{100}$ sevens.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{7}&\overline{\Big)741}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{700}}&\purpleD{100}&\maroonD{7} \times \purpleD{100} = \blueE{700}\\
&41
\end{array}$

We have $41$ left.

Let's divide $41$ into $\purpleD{5}$ more sevens.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{7}&\overline{\Big)741}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{700}&100\\
&41\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{35}}&\purpleD{5}&\maroonD{7} \times \purpleD{5} = \blueE{35}\\
&6
\end{array}$

We have $6$ left.  That is less than $7$, so we'll stop dividing.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{7}&\overline{\Big)741}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{700}&\purpleD{100}\\
&41\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{35}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{5}}\\
&\goldE{6}&\greenE{105}
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{100} + \purpleD{5}= \greenE{105}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{6}$.

$\begin{align}
\greenE{105}&\text{ remainder }\goldE{6}\\
7\overline{\Big)741}&
\end{align}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $8$ that may be useful:

- $8 \times 4 = 32$
- $8 \times 6 = 48$
- $8 \times 10 = 80$

Let's start by dividing $4{,}832$ into $\purpleD{600}$ eights.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{8}&\overline{\Big)4{,}832}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{4{,}800}}&\purpleD{600}&\maroonD{8} \times \purpleD{600} = \blueE{4{,}800}\\
&32
\end{array}$

We have $32$ left.

Let's divide $32$ into $\purpleD{4}$ more eights since  $4\times8=32$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{8}&\overline{\Big)4{,}832}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{4{,}800}&600\\
&32\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{32}}&\purpleD{4}&\maroonD{8} \times \purpleD{4} = \blueE{32}\\
&0
\end{array}$

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{8}&\overline{\Big)4{,}832}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{4{,}800}&\purpleD{600}\\
&32\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{32}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{4}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{604}
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{600} + \purpleD{4}= \greenE{604}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{0}$.
$4{,}832 \div 8 =\greenE{604}$ remainder $\goldE{0}$

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $6$ that may be useful:

- $6 \times 7 = 42$
- $6 \times 9 = 54$
- $6 \times 10 = 60$

Let's start by dividing $543$ into $\purpleD{90}$ sixes.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{6}&\overline{\Big)543}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{540}}&\purpleD{90}&\maroonD{6} \times \purpleD{90} = \blueE{540}\\
&3\\
\end{array}$

We have $3$ left.  That is less than $6$, so we'll stop dividing.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Now, let's add all of the partial quotients (there is only one this time) to find our total quotient. 

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{6}&\overline{\Big)543}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{540}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{90}}\\
&\goldE{3}&\greenE{90}
\end{array}$

The quotient is $\greenE{90}$. There is a remainder of $\goldE{3}$.
$543 \div 6 =\greenE{90}$ remainder $\goldE{3}$
